title: Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS)
principle: Keep untrusted input away from regexes that can backtrack exponentially. Avoid nested or overlapping quantifiers like (a+)+ or (.*)*; anchor patterns, bound repetition, and prefer a linear-time engine or plain string operations. If a user supplies the pattern, don't run it at all.

snippets.library: a bounded, non-backtracking pattern (or the 're2' package)
snippets.secure:
if (/^\w+(\s\w+)*$/.test(input)) { /* no overlapping quantifiers */ }
// for user-supplied patterns use node-re2, which runs in linear time
snippets.note: The danger is nested/adjacent quantifiers that can match the same text many ways. RE2 (via the re2 npm package) has no backtracking and can't ReDoS.

snippets.library: the 're2' module for untrusted input, or a bounded pattern
snippets.secure:
re.match(r'^a+$', user_input)   # flatten the nested quantifier
# or: import re2; re2.match(pattern, user_input)  # linear time
snippets.note: Python's re backtracks. Google's re2 module is linear-time and safe for user-supplied patterns or hostile input. Also cap input length.

snippets.library: a possessive quantifier / bounded pattern, or RE2/J
snippets.secure:
Pattern.compile("a++").matcher(input).matches();   // possessive: no backtracking
// or use com.google.re2j.Pattern for untrusted patterns
snippets.note: Possessive quantifiers (a++, a*+) don't backtrack. RE2/J runs in linear time and is the safe choice for attacker-controlled input.

snippets.vulnerable: // importing a PCRE-style engine and using (a+)+ on user input
snippets.secure:
re := regexp.MustCompile(`a+`)
re.MatchString(userInput)   // Go's regexp is RE2: no catastrophic backtracking
snippets.note: Go's standard regexp package is RE2 and cannot ReDoS by construction. The risk only appears if you pull in a backtracking PCRE binding.

references.title: OWASP Regular expression Denial of Service (ReDoS)
